section.title: The Journey to Jerusalem and Paul's Arrest
section.sectionRationale: Paul sails resolutely toward Jerusalem though the Spirit and the disciples repeatedly warn of chains. In Jerusalem James urges him to join four men in a temple vow to quiet rumors that he teaches Jews to forsake Moses; instead a mob from Asia seizes him on a false charge, and the Roman commander arrests him, opening the long chain of trials that will carry the gospel to Rome.
section.thesis: Set on finishing his course whatever it costs, Paul goes to Jerusalem, takes up James's counsel of a vow to show he honors his people, and is seized on a false accusation, so that his arrest becomes the doorway through which he will testify before governors, kings, and finally in Rome.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the voyage down the coast to Caesarea; Agabus's warning and Paul's resolve; James's counsel and the temple vow; the riot and seizure at the temple; and the Roman rescue with permission to speak.

units.label: The voyage to Caesarea
units.note: warned through the Spirit, farewells on the shore

verses.text: After we had torn ourselves away from them, we sailed directly to Cos, and the next day on to Rhodes, and from there to Patara.
Finding a ship crossing over to Phoenicia, we boarded it and set sail.
After sighting Cyprus and passing south of it, we sailed on to Syria and landed at Tyre, where the ship was to unload its cargo.
We sought out the disciples in Tyre and stayed with them seven days. Through the Spirit they kept telling Paul not to go up to Jerusalem.
But when our time there had ended, we set out on our journey. All the disciples, with their wives and children, accompanied us out of the city and knelt down on the beach to pray with us.
And after we had said our farewells, we went aboard the ship, and they returned home.
When we had finished our voyage from Tyre, we landed at Ptolemais, where we greeted the brothers and stayed with them for a day.
Leaving the next day, we went on to Caesarea and stayed at the home of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the Seven.
He had four unmarried daughters who prophesied.

units.label: Agabus and the resolve to go

verses.text: After we had been there several days, a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.

Coming over to us, he took Paul’s belt, bound his own feet and hands, and said, “The Holy Spirit says: ‘In this way the Jews of Jerusalem will bind the owner of this belt and hand him over to the Gentiles.’”
When we heard this, we and the people there pleaded with Paul not to go up to Jerusalem.
Then Paul answered, “Why are you weeping and breaking my heart? I am ready not only to be bound, but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.”
When he would not be dissuaded, we quieted down and said, “The Lord’s will be done.”
After these days, we packed up and went on to Jerusalem.
Some of the disciples from Caesarea accompanied us, and they took us to stay at the home of Mnason the Cypriot, an early disciple.

units.label: James's counsel and the temple vow

verses.text: When we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers welcomed us joyfully.
The next day Paul went in with us to see James, and all the elders were present.
Paul greeted them and recounted one by one the things that God had done among the {{ethnos|Gentiles}} through his ministry.
When they heard this, they glorified God. Then they said to Paul, “You see, brother, how many thousands of Jews have believed, and all of them are {{zelotes|zealous}} for the law.
But they are under the impression that you teach all the Jews who live among the Gentiles to forsake Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children or observe our customs.
What then should we do? They will certainly hear that you have come.
Therefore do what we advise you. There are four men with us who have taken a {{euche|vow}}.

Take these men, {{hagnizo|purify}} yourself along with them, and pay their expenses so they can have their heads shaved. Then everyone will know that there is no truth to these rumors about you, but that you also live in obedience to the law.
As for the Gentile believers, we have written to them our decision that they must abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality.”

So the next day Paul took the men and purified himself along with them. Then he entered the temple to give notice of the date when their purification would be complete and the offering would be made for each of them.

units.label: Seized at the temple
units.note: a false charge, a riot, an arrest

verses.text: When the seven days were almost over, some Jews from the province of Asia saw Paul at the temple. They stirred up the whole crowd and seized him,

crying out, “Men of Israel, help us! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people and against our law and against this place. Furthermore, he has brought Greeks into the temple and defiled this holy place.”
For they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian with him in the city, and they assumed that Paul had brought him into the temple.
The whole city was stirred up, and the people rushed together. They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple, and at once the gates were shut.
While they were trying to kill him, the commander of the Roman regiment received a report that all Jerusalem was in turmoil.
Immediately he took some soldiers and centurions and ran down to the crowd. When the people saw the commander and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
The commander came up and arrested Paul, ordering that he be bound with two chains. Then he asked who he was and what he had done.
Some in the crowd were shouting one thing, and some another. And since the commander could not get at the truth because of the uproar, he ordered that Paul be brought into the barracks.
When Paul reached the steps, he had to be carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the mob.
For the crowd that followed him kept shouting, “Away with him!”

units.label: Permission to speak
units.note: a citizen of no ordinary city

verses.text: As they were about to take Paul into the barracks, he asked the commander, “May I say something to you?” “Do you speak Greek?” he replied.
“Aren’t you the Egyptian who incited a rebellion some time ago and led four thousand members of the Assassins into the wilderness?”
But Paul answered, “I am a Jew from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no ordinary city. Now I beg you to allow me to speak to the people.”
Having received permission, Paul stood on the steps and motioned to the crowd. A great hush came over the crowd, and he addressed them in Hebrew:

ethnos.gloss: Nation, people; in the plural, the Gentiles, the nations outside Israel. Paul reports what God has done among the ethnē, the very fruit of the mission that Jerusalem struggles to reconcile with zeal for the law.

zelotes.gloss: One who is zealous, a zealot. Thousands of Jewish believers are zealous for the law of Moses; their zeal is real but easily inflamed, and it lies behind the fear that Paul is teaching Jews to abandon their heritage.

euche.gloss: A vow or solemn promise to God, here likely a Nazirite vow with its shaved head and offerings, Numbers 6. James proposes that Paul join and pay for four such men to show publicly that he still honors the law.

hagnizo.gloss: To purify, make ceremonially clean. Paul purifies himself with the four men in the temple, a costly act of goodwill toward Jewish believers; ironically it is in the temple, at the point of this obedience, that he is falsely accused of defiling it.

basic.context: Paul sails down the coast toward Jerusalem, and everywhere the Spirit warns of chains. Friends beg him to turn back; the prophet Agabus acts out his coming arrest with Paul's own belt. Paul will not be moved: he is ready to die for the name of Jesus. In Jerusalem, James welcomes him but reports a rumor that Paul tells Jews to abandon Moses, and urges him to join four men in a temple vow to prove it false. Paul agrees, but a mob from Asia seizes him on a lie, and the Romans arrest him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative that shows more than it commands. Watch two threads: Paul's settled resolve to obey God whatever the cost, and the tangle of law, rumor, and mob that surrounds him. The chapter does not tell us in so many words whether the vow was wise; it lets us weigh it, while making plain that God is steering even the arrest toward His purpose.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul reports what God did among the ethnē, the Gentiles. The Jerusalem believers are zelotai, zealots for the law. James proposes that Paul share a euchē, a vow, and hagnizesthai, purify himself, in the temple. The very act meant to prove his loyalty becomes the setting where enemies accuse him of defiling the holy place.
basic.typology: Paul, bound and handed over to the Gentiles just as Agabus foretold, walks a road that echoes his Lord: Jesus too was warned of, resolved to go up to Jerusalem, and was handed over to the Gentiles. The disciple follows the Master's pattern, though only Christ's binding could atone.
basic.christ: Paul says he is ready to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus. His resolve is not stoic pride but love for Christ and His mission. As Jesus set His face to go to Jerusalem knowing what awaited Him, so Paul goes, and his sufferings will become the means of carrying Christ's name to Rome, just as Christ said they would.
basic.god: God had said Paul would carry His name before Gentiles and kings, and He is now bringing it to pass through a riot and an arrest. The Spirit's warnings are not commands to stop but preparation to stand firm. When friends finally say, The Lord's will be done, they name the truth that governs the whole chapter.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 9:51 shows Jesus setting His face to Jerusalem. Acts 9:15-16 promised Paul would suffer for the name. Numbers 6 gives the Nazirite vow behind the temple rite. Acts 23:11 confirms that God intends this path to reach Rome.
basic.oneStory: The gospel is pressing from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, and here the road narrows to a prison chain. Paul's arrest looks like defeat, but it is the hinge God uses to send His witness before rulers and, at last, to Rome. The mission advances not around suffering but straight through it.

context.literary: The chapter continues a we passage and moves the story from the churches of the mission field to Jerusalem, setting up the trials that fill the rest of Acts. Agabus's acted prophecy recalls the sign-acts of the Old Testament prophets, and the temple riot mirrors the charges once leveled at Stephen and at Jesus.
context.historical: Around AD 57, Jerusalem held many thousands of Jewish believers still devoted to temple and law. Gentiles could enter only the outer court; a stone barrier warned them, on pain of death, not to pass into the inner courts. The rumor that Paul had brought a Greek inside touched the most explosive nerve in the city.
context.canonical: This is the pivot of the last quarter of Acts. From here Paul is a prisoner, and his chains become the platform for defense speeches before crowd, council, governors, and king. The chapter shows the young church wrestling with how Jewish believers and Gentile believers belong together in one people of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke shows Paul's unshakable resolve to obey God at any cost, the goodwill he showed to Jewish believers, and the false charge that led to his arrest, so that readers see God turning even a riot into the advance of the gospel toward Rome.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The travel, the vow, and the riot describe what happened; they do not prescribe a rite for us. The abiding lessons are Paul's courage under warning, his readiness to accommodate others for the gospel's sake, and God's sovereignty over suffering.

debates.question: Was Paul right to take the temple vow at James's urging?

views.label: Yes, a wise and loving accommodation
views.case: Paul had written that to the Jews he became as a Jew to win them, 1 Corinthians 9:20, and the vow denied no gospel truth; it simply showed he did not despise his heritage. The strongest case is that Paul acted in love to protect the unity of Jewish and Gentile believers, and Luke records no divine rebuke.

views.label: No, an unwise compromise
views.case: Some judge that Paul, the apostle of freedom from the law, bent too far to pressure and blurred the very liberty Galatians defends, and that the plan failed and landed him in chains. The strongest case is that the scheme did not achieve its aim and that appeasing the zealots risked confusing the gospel.

debates.bearing: How we read the vow shapes how we handle Christian liberty: whether we may freely accommodate scruples for the sake of peace and mission, as long as no gospel truth is surrendered, or whether such accommodation endangers the freedom Christ won.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a clash between mission and zeal. Paul recounts what God did among the ethnē, the Gentiles, the harvest of grace. But Jerusalem's believers are zelotai, zealous for the law, and fear Paul undermines Moses. James proposes a euchē, a vow, and that Paul hagnizesthai, purify himself, in the temple, a public sign of respect for the law. The irony is sharp: at the very moment of ritual purity, enemies charge him with defiling the holy place, and the accommodation ends in chains.

typology.type: Bound and handed to the Gentiles
typology.fulfillment: Agabus foretells Paul being bound and handed over, echoing Christ who was bound and delivered to the Gentiles, Luke 18:32; the servant follows the Master's road of suffering.

typology.type: The false charge before the mob
typology.fulfillment: As false witnesses accused Stephen and Jesus of speaking against temple and law, so Paul is charged; the pattern of the rejected witness runs from Christ through His church.
The friends' surrender echoes Christ's own Not my will but Yours, Luke 22:42, the posture that carries the mission through suffering.

study.christ: Paul declares he is ready to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus, and his path deliberately mirrors his Lord's: warned of what awaits, resolved to go up, bound, and handed to the Gentiles. Yet the parallel only underlines the difference, for Christ's binding purchased salvation, while Paul's serves to spread the news of it. The name Paul suffers for is the name at the center of the whole book, and God will use these chains to carry it to Rome.
study.god: God governs every step. He had promised Paul would suffer for the name and would witness to kings; the Spirit's repeated warnings prepare him to stand rather than to flee. Even the riot and the Roman arrest are within God's hand, the means by which His witness will be brought before rulers. The chapter's quiet climax is the church's confession, The Lord's will be done.

crossReferences.note: Jesus set His face to go to Jerusalem, the pattern of resolute obedience Paul now follows.
The Lord's word that Paul would carry His name before Gentiles and kings and suffer for it, now being fulfilled.
Paul's principle of becoming as a Jew to the Jews, the likely rationale for accepting the temple vow.
The Lord stands by Paul and confirms he must testify in Rome, showing the arrest as God's chosen road.

study.oneStory: The gospel that God is driving from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth here passes through a riot and a chain. Paul goes up resolved to obey whatever it costs, tries to keep peace with his zealous kinsmen, and is seized on a lie. What looks like the end of his mission is really its next stage, for through these chains God will bring His witness before governors and kings and at last to Rome, exactly as He said.

quiz.q: Why did Paul keep going to Jerusalem despite the warnings?

options.text: He was ready to be bound and to die for the name of the Lord Jesus.
options.note: Paul says exactly this in verse 13, showing his resolve to obey whatever the cost.

options.text: He wanted to report to James and the elders what God had done.
options.note: He did report to James, but his stated reason for pressing on was his readiness to suffer for Christ's name.

options.text: He hoped the warnings would prove exaggerated.
options.note: The reverse: he took the warnings seriously and went anyway, ready for chains and death.

options.text: He was under Roman orders to appear in the city.
options.note: No such order existed; his arrest by Rome comes only after he arrives.

quiz.q: Why did James urge Paul to join the four men in a temple vow?

options.text: To show publicly that Paul still honored the law and had not told Jews to forsake Moses.
options.note: James names this aim: to disprove the rumor and show Paul lives in obedience to the law.

options.text: To keep peace among the thousands of Jewish believers zealous for the law.
options.note: True and part of the motive, but the specific purpose was to refute the rumor about Paul by a public sign.

options.text: To require Gentile believers to keep the whole law of Moses.
options.note: The opposite: James reaffirms the earlier decision that Gentiles keep only a few essentials.

options.text: To punish Paul for preaching to the Gentiles.
options.note: James and the elders glorified God for Paul's Gentile ministry; the vow was not a punishment.

quiz.q: What was the accusation that sparked the riot?

options.text: That Paul taught against the people and the law and had brought a Greek into the temple.
options.note: The Asian Jews charged him on both counts, the temple charge based on a false assumption about Trophimus.

options.text: That Paul had brought a Gentile past the barrier into the temple.
options.note: This was the inflaming charge, but the accusers also charged him with teaching against people, law, and place.

options.text: That Paul had stolen from the temple treasury.
options.note: No theft is charged; the accusation concerned teaching and defiling the temple.

options.text: That Paul had denied that Jesus is the Messiah.
options.note: The charge was the opposite sort; nothing here concerns denying Christ.

quiz.q: How does the chapter show God's hand in Paul's arrest?

options.text: The arrest fulfills the warnings and becomes the road by which Paul will witness to rulers and reach Rome.
options.note: The Spirit foretold the chains, and Acts shows them as God's chosen path to Rome.

options.text: The Roman commander rescued Paul from the mob just in time.
options.note: True and providential, but it understates the larger design: the arrest itself serves God's mission.

options.text: God kept Paul from any suffering at all.
options.note: Paul was beaten and bound; God's purpose ran through the suffering, not around it.

options.text: The arrest ended Paul's usefulness for the gospel.
options.note: The opposite: it opened his greatest platform, before governors, a king, and finally Rome.

quiz.q: How did the friends respond when Paul would not be dissuaded?

options.text: They stopped urging him and said, The Lord's will be done.
options.note: They surrendered to God's will, echoing Christ's own prayer in Gethsemane.

options.text: They wept and pleaded with him not to go up to Jerusalem.
options.note: They did weep and plead at first, but their final word was submission to the Lord's will.

options.text: They abandoned Paul and refused to travel with him.
options.note: The reverse: some disciples accompanied him on to Jerusalem.

options.text: They forced Paul to change his plans.
options.note: Paul could not be dissuaded, and they yielded to him and to God's will.

questions: Paul was ready not only to be bound but to die for the name of Jesus. What would it look like for you to hold your own life loosely for the sake of Christ?
The disciples' warnings came through the Spirit, yet Paul still went. How do you discern between a warning about hardship and a command to turn back?
James asked Paul to accommodate the scruples of Jewish believers without surrendering the gospel. Where might love call you to yield your freedom for the sake of others?
The very act meant to prove Paul's goodwill led to his arrest. How do you keep trusting God when doing the right thing seems to make matters worse?
When they could not change Paul's mind, the friends prayed, The Lord's will be done. Where do you need to come to that same rest today?
Paul's chains became the road to Rome. Where might God be working through a hardship in your life in a way you cannot yet see?

translationNotes.note: Addressed them in Hebrew carries a footnoted alternative, in Aramaic; literally in the Hebrew language. It names the local language of the Jerusalem crowd.
The vow with its shaved heads and offerings is best understood as a Nazirite vow, Numbers 6; Paul pays the expenses of the four men to help them complete it.
The Assassins renders the term for the sicarii, dagger-carrying rebels of the period; the commander first mistakes Paul for a known Egyptian insurgent.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 21. Footnote markers and line breaks (including the inline lineBreak in verse 37) were skipped when building verse text; text elements were joined with single spaces. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
